title: Normal calculations in reverse
description: Practice finding a value in a normal density curve given an area.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of SAT scores of all college-bound seniors taking the SAT in 2014 was approximately normal with mean $ \mu = 1497 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 322 $.

A certain summer program only admits students whose SAT scores are in the top $15$ percent of those who take the test in a given year. 

**What is the minimum SAT score in $2014$ that meets the program’s requirements?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 1497 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 322 $.

We want to find the SAT score such that the area under the curve to its right is $0.15$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, SAT score = unknown. The axis ranges from 209 to 2785 in increments of 322. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 209 with increasing steepness to its peak at 1497, before falling with decreasing steepness through 2785, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 1820 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.15.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6af1530f3a5cb2de5f4a08405b98509c6475489f)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.15$ above it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 1.04 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.15.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a58b3bdc0d85b09bb9c0ce371ff85e00bba99612)

Knowing that the $z$-score with an area of $0.15$ above it is the same as the $z$-score with an area of $0.85$ below it, we can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 0.9, 1.0, and 1.1, and 3 columns labeled, 0.03, 0.04, and 0.05. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.8238, 0.8264, 0.8289. Row 2. 0.8485, 0.8508, 0.8531. Row 3. 0.8708, 0.8729, 0.8749. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f72441d2151073854e62d92414d3329d1bc35bca)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 1.04. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 1.04 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.8508.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7932299ec9a27fcb24ec23785a9199dbf5a895c8)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx 1.04$ to guarantee that we have $85\%$ of data below that point so we are in the top $15\%$.

Now we can find the SAT score that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=1.04$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
1.04 &= \dfrac {x-1497} {322} \\\\
x &= 1831.88
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.85 \\\\
&\mu:1497 \\\\
&\sigma:322 \\\\
x&=1830.73
\end{align}$

The answer:

The minimum SAT score in $2014$ that meets the program’s requirement is approximately $1831$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of resting pulse rates of all students at Santa Maria high school was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 80$  beats per minute and standard deviation $\sigma = 9$ beats per minute.

The school nurse plans to provide additional screening to students whose resting pulse rates are in the top $30\%$ of the students who were tested.

**What is the minimum resting pulse rate at that school for students who will receive additional screening?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 80 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 9 $.

We want to find the resting pulse rate such that the area under the curve to its right is $0.3$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Resting pulse rate = unknown. The axis ranges from 44 to 116 in increments of 9. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 44 with increasing steepness to its peak at 80, before falling with decreasing steepness through 116, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 84.5 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/038a1f9e6b0593c010821a0297d5d10d2c68397f)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.3$ above it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 0.5 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2ec1913395482df654afae1b01b8eff79a811495)

Knowing that the $z$-score with an area of $0.3$ above it is the same as the $z$-score with an area of $0.7$ below it, we can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 0.4, 0.5, and 0.6, and 3 columns labeled, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.6628, 0.6664, 0.67. Row 2. 0.6985, 0.7019, 0.7054. Row 3. 0.7324, 0.7357, 0.7389. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d162e782a38b46820f657d25e7b2358479916b58)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 0.53. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 0.53 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.7019.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/eef82d7bb42731747d19d23a901f63b3a9ee3bd2)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx 0.53$ to guarantee that we have $70\%$ of data below that point so we are in the top $30\%$.

Now we can find the resting pulse rate that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=0.53$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
0.53 &= \dfrac {x-80} {9} \\\\
x &= 84.77
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.70 \\\\
&\mu:80 \\\\
&\sigma:9 \\\\
x&=84.7196
\end{align}$

The answer:

The minimum resting pulse rate at that school for students who will receive additional screening is approximately $85$ beats per minute.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of average wait times in drive-through restaurant lines in one town was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 185$ seconds and standard deviation $\sigma = 11$ seconds.

A journalist wrote an article about the restaurants whose average drive-through wait times were in the top $25\%$ for that town.

**What is the minimum average wait time for restaurants that the journalist included in the article?**  
*Round to the nearest whole second.* 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 185 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 11 $.

We want to find the wait time such that the area under the curve to its right is $0.25$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Wait time, in seconds, = unknown. The axis ranges from 141 to 229 in increments of 11. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 141 with increasing steepness to its peak at 185, before falling with decreasing steepness through 229, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 190 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.25.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d3ebcb334c2b93cd682ae548a000a9c8889c2c92)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.25$ above it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 0.68 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.25.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6c90610892f34779606e163b158871dfb25cbe92)

Knowing that the $z$-score with an area of $0.25$ above it is the same as the $z$-score with an area of $0.75$ below it, we can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 0.5, 0.6, and 0.7, and 3 columns labeled, 0.07, 0.08, and 0.09. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.7157, 0.719, 0.7224. Row 2. 0.7486, 0.7517, 0.7549. Row 3. 0.7794, 0.7823, 0.7852. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5f44ca0ad152f75826c640e0e140cccf9a8849a9)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 0.68. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 0.68 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.7517.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e49f1fb00e10b6db99da97466af174dfcf75ba84)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx 0.68$ to guarantee that we have $75\%$ of data below that point so we are in the top $25\%$.

Now we can find the wait time that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=0.68$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
0.68 &= \dfrac {x-185} {11} \\\\
x &= 192.48
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.75 \\\\
&\mu:185 \\\\
&\sigma:11 \\\\
x&=192.419
\end{align}$

The answer:

The minimum average wait time for restaurants that the journalist included in the article is approximately $193$ seconds.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of reading scale scores in the $4^\text{th}$ grade at Roosevelt Elementary School was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 221$  and standard deviation $\sigma = 36$.

Students must score in the top $20\%$ to be eligible for participation in the gifted program.

**What is the minimum reading scale score for students to be eligible for the gifted program?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 221 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 36 $.

We want to find the reading scale score such that the area under the curve to its right is $0.2$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Reading scale score = unknown. The axis ranges from 77 to 365 in increments of 36. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 77 with increasing steepness to its peak at 221, before falling with decreasing steepness through 365, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 250 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ea44acc0f0c97dc930b877c46edcc538769ca3e4)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.2$ above it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 0.85 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4d6a1559045fd688a5f98d022b059905e9968f3b)

Knowing that the $z$-score with an area of $0.2$ above it is the same as the $z$-score with an area of $0.8$ below it, we can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 0.7, 0.8, and 0.9, and 3 columns labeled, 0.04, 0.05. and 0.06. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.7704, 0.7734, 0.7764. Row 2. 0.7995, 0.8023, 0.8051. Row 3. 0.8264, 0.8289, 0.8315.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/93b2b47ed806229f79d0773c7bfb4e979abd9d19)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 0.85. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 0.85 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.8023.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6a2ea5aa088d50936bc1f1d1fb0abad8e6a9df3c)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx 0.85$ to guarantee that we have $80\%$ of data below that point so  we are in the top $20\%$.

Now we can find the reading scale score that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=0.85$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
0.85 &= \dfrac {x-221} {36} \\\\
x &= 251.6
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.80 \\\\
&\mu:221 \\\\
&\sigma:36 \\\\
x&=251.298
\end{align}$

The answer:

The minimum reading scale score for students to be eligible for the gifted program is approximately $252$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of annual profit at a chain of stores was approximately normal with mean $\mu = \$66{,}000$  and standard deviation $\sigma = \$22{,}000$.

The stores with profits in the top $5\%$ each had a reward party for the employees to celebrate.

**What is closest to the minimum annual profit for a store that had a reward party?**  
*Round to the nearest thousand dollars.*

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 66{,}000 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 22{,}000 $.

We want to find the annual profit such that the area under the curve to its right is $0.05$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Profits, in thousands of dollars, = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 18 to 150 in increments of 21. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 18 with increasing steepness to its peak at 66, before falling with decreasing steepness through 150, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 100 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/72895ecc16348e50e399d6c69bf7d33d04b56ac1)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.05$ above it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 1.65 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/760392e4b94d3efceb089b97ad707209849602ee)

Knowing that the $z$-score with an area of $0.05$ above it is the same as the $z$-score with an area of $0.95$ below it, we can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 1.5, 1.6, and 1.7, and 3 columns labeled, 0.04, 0.05, and 0.06. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.9382, 0.9394, 0.9406. Row 2. 0.9495, 0.9505, 0.9515. Row 3. 0.9591, 0.9599, 0.9608. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/06165f19d0727cef3db6bc0c149bd6df0dd6af48)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 1.65. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 1.65 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.9505.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9dadac084a342ebc496f916bf8d4104f571ae73c)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx 1.65$ to guarantee that we have $95\%$ of data below that point so we are in the top $5\%$.

Now we can find the annual profit that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=1.65$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
1.65 &= \dfrac {x-66{,}000} {22{,}000} \\\\
x &= 102{,}300
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.95 \\\\
&\mu:66000 \\\\
&\sigma:22000 \\\\
x&= 102186.78
\end{align}$

The answer:

The minimum annual profit for a store that had a reward party is approximately $\$102{,}000$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of waist circumferences of US adult men randomly selected for a research study was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 94$ centimeters and standard deviation $\sigma = 11$ centimeters.

A clothing store that specializes in serving larger men aims to sell clothing that will fit men with the top $35\%$ of waist circumferences.

**What is the minimum waist circumference of men whom the store aims to serve?**  
*Round to the nearest whole centimeter.*  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 94 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 11 $.

We want to find the waist circumference such that the area under the curve to its right is $0.35$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Waist circumference, in centimeters, = unknown. The axis ranges from 50 to 138 in increments of 11. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 50 with increasing steepness to its peak at 94, before falling with decreasing steepness through 138, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 98 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.35.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/76dc9a110557e5f3e174ac0bb3bc4db1299049d5)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.35$ above it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 0.35 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.35.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/717b958d167f3690da9f87f9852f370235474e23)

Knowing that the $z$-score with an area of $0.35$ above it is the same as the $z$-score with an area of $0.65$ below it, we can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4, and 3 columns labeled, 0.07, 0.08, and 0.09. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.6064, 0.6103, 0.6141. Row 2. 0.6443, 0.6480, 0.6517. Row 3. 0.6808, 0.6844, 0.6879. Row 2 and column 3 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/dc63f1a0f787c6c55befe833b338a5ea04e4447e)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 0.39. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 0.35 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.6517.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae3d13657c2c210421b2a99b58a65d81d23cf198)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx 0.39$ to guarantee that we have $65\%$ of data below that point so we are in the top $35\%$.

Now we can find the waist circumference that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=0.39$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
0.39 &= \dfrac {x-94} {11} \\\\
x &= 98.29
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.65 \\\\
&\mu:94 \\\\
&\sigma:11 \\\\
x&=98.2385
\end{align}$

The answer:

The minimum waist circumference of men whom the store aims to serve is approximately $99$ centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of durations for which apartments remain empty after the resident moves out for one property management company over the past $10$ years was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 85$ days and standard deviation $\sigma = 29$ days.

The property management company intends to update the kitchen appliances in the apartments that were empty for top $10\%$ of durations.

**What is the minimum duration for which an apartment remained empty for the company to update the kitchen appliances?**  
*Round to the nearest whole number.*  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 85 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 29 $.

We want to find the duration such that the area under the curve to its right is $0.1$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Days empty = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 31 to 201 in increments of 29. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 31 with increasing steepness to its peak at 85, before falling with decreasing steepness through 201, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 120 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ddb15ea556701a0b89a7c6024346bf35b28448db)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.1$ above it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 1.25 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, rightward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/101c6e6b32d3d435066aaf36aecb0b77a1cf8dc9)

Knowing that the $z$-score with an area of $0.1$ above it is the same as the $z$-score with an area of $0.9$ below it, we can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 1.1, 1.2, and 1.3, and 3 columns labeled, .08, .09, and .1. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.881, 0.883, 0.8849. Row 2. 0.8997, 09.015, 09032. Row 3. 09162, 09177, 09192. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3560460e017d366dde56c04af78d238604db8e35)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 1.29. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 1.25 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.9015.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0ea7a523da3cac2bb2d0fbdc994ebf9eead2e645)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx 1.29$ to guarantee that we have $90\%$ of data below that point so we are in the top $10\%$.

Now we can find the duration that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=1.29$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
1.29 &= \dfrac {x-85} {29} \\\\
x &= 122.41
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.90 \\\\
&\mu:85 \\\\
&\sigma:29 \\\\
x&=122.165
\end{align}$

The answer:

The minimum duration for which an apartment remained empty for the company to update the kitchen appliances is approximately $123$ days.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of SAT scores of all college-bound seniors taking the SAT in 2014 was approximately normal with mean $ \mu = 1497 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 322 $.

A certain test-retake preparation course is designed for students whose SAT scores are in the lower $25\%$ of those who take the test in a given year.

**What is the maximum SAT score in $2014$ that meets the course requirements?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 1497 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 322 $.

We want to find the SAT score such that the area under the curve to its left is $0.25$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, SAT score, = unknown. The axis ranges from 209 to 2785 in increments of 322. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 209 with increasing steepness to its peak at 1497, before falling with decreasing steepness through 2785, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 1250 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.25.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f6c44fa9a218065a59356f0f96d31766e20eb07)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.25$ below it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately negative 0.68 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.25.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/845df9c294b14d5f4cc0493da6bbf5747452a6d8)

We can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, negative 0.7, negative 0.6, and negative 0.5, and columns labeled, 0.7, 0.8, and 0.9. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.2206, 0.2177, 0.2148. Row 2. 0.2514, 0.2483, 0.2451. Row 3. 0.2843, 0.281, 0.2776. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c6bae2cdb1a5716e8ad269d3b3112b162854ee51)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = negative 0.68. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at negative 0.68 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.2483.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d2882a54998073693754475dbeabfb8e99fb93bd)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx -0.68$ to guarantee that we are in the bottom $25\%$.

Now we can find the SAT score that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=-0.68$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
-0.68 &= \dfrac {x-1497} {322} \\\\
x &= 1278.04
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.25 \\\\
&\mu:1497 \\\\
&\sigma:322 \\\\
x&=1279.81
\end{align}$

The answer:

The maximum SAT score in $2014$ that meets the course requirements is approximately $1279$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of resting pulse rates of all students at Adams High School was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 80$ beats per minute and standard deviation $\sigma = 9$ beats per minute.

Only students whose resting pulse rates are in the lower $40\%$ are eligible to join the weight-lifting club.

**What is the maximum resting pulse rate for students who are eligible to join the weight-lifting club?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 80 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 9 $.

We want to find the resting pulse rate such that the area under the curve to its left is $0.4$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Resting pulse rate = unknown. The axis ranges from 44 to 116 in increments of 9. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 44 with increasing steepness to its peak at 80, before falling with decreasing steepness through 116, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 77 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9958643622c6e189b4da66b2df156e5fa4b433a3)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.4$ below it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately negative 0.20 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.4.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/983cf67113bd881a3d3862d18986c583e3a0b699)

We can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, negative 0.3, negative 0.2, and negative 0.1, and 3 columns labeled, .05, .06, and .07. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.3632, 0.3594, 0.3557. Row 2. 0.4013, 0.3974, 0.3936. Row 3. 0.4404, 0.4364, 0.4325. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d0125e30a6124aed4540fcada89de649b8f9ff91)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = negative 0.26. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at negative 0.26 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.3974.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5cb666251ef042b5d2b24540dae6c3dad430ea9b)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx -0.26$ to guarantee that we are in the bottom $40\%$.

Now we can find the resting pulse rate that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=-0.26$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
-0.26 &= \dfrac {x-80} {9} \\\\
x &= 77.66
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.40 \\\\
&\mu:80 \\\\
&\sigma:9 \\\\
x&=77.7199
\end{align}$

The answer:

The maximum resting pulse rate for students who are eligible to join the weight-lifting club is approximately $77$ beats per minute.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of average wait times in drive-through restaurant lines in one town was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 185$ seconds and standard deviation $\sigma = 11$ seconds.

Amelia only likes to use the drive-through for restaurants where the average wait time is in the bottom $10\%$ for that town.

**What is the maximum average wait time for restaurants where Amelia likes to use the drive-through?**  
*Round to the nearest whole second.*  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 185 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 11 $.

We want to find the wait time such that the area under the curve to its left is $0.1$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Wait time, in seconds, = unknown. The axis ranges from 141 to 229 in increments of 11. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 141 with increasing steepness to its peak at 185, before falling with decreasing steepness through 229, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 170 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a84ee789e91a066a5411e0648c35a8a4236940e5)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.1$ below it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately negative 1.29 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.1.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/78101133817956b308582b91de7faa48238b2274)

We can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, negative 1.3, negative 1.2, and negative 1.1, and 3 columns labeled, .08, .09, and .1. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.0838, 0.0823, 0.0808. Row 2. 0.1003, 0.0985, 0.0968. Row 3. 0.119, 0.117, 0.1151. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5518f9aee004a4f9edadd41f77c1e10e9e4861c0)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = negative 1.29. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at negative 1.29 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.0985.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/47cc8bb8c9a202548b6bc780cd4c86807fd2bf02)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx -1.29$ to guarantee that we are in the bottom $10\%$.

Now we can find the wait time that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=-1.29$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
-1.29 &= \dfrac {x-185} {11} \\\\
x &= 170.81
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.10 \\\\
&\mu:185 \\\\
&\sigma:11 \\\\
x&=170.903
\end{align}$

The answer:

The maximum average wait time for restaurants where Amelia likes to use the drive-through is approximately $170$ seconds.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of reading scale scores in the $4^\text{th}$ grade at Truman Elementary School was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 221$  and standard deviation $\sigma = 36$.

Educational researchers are conducting an intervention experiment. To participate in the experiment, a student must have a reading scale score in the lower $50\%$ of the scores in their grade level.

**What is the maximum reading scale score for students to participate in the intervention experiment?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 221 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 36 $.

We want to find the reading scale score such that the area under the curve to its left is $0.5$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Reading scale score = unknown. The axis ranges from 77 to 365 in increments of 36. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at 77 with increasing steepness to its peak at 221, before falling with decreasing steepness through 365, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 221 to the peak of the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of 221 is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/64b71c4e505d1adb1cc98e1a0eda23d8e33ad989)

In a normal distribution, the mean and the median are the same, so the median reading scale score is also $221$. This means that $50\%$ of the students will have scored at or below $221$.

We can confirm this by considering the $z$-score with an area of $0.5$ below it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 0 to the peak of the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of 0 is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/289862274466e31730aa5981952d1df84981ff23)

We can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, 0, 0.1, and 0.2, and 3 columns labeled,0, 0.01, and 0.02. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.5, 0.504, 0.508. Row 2. 0.5398, 0.5438, 0.5478. Row 3. 0.5793, 0.5832, 0.5871. Row 1 and column 1 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/17abbdee06a84a1e8ebef4e20b6a82b91b6df8aa)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = 0. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at 0 to the peak of the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of 0 is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.5.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cf9a5133ff4999b6298cbc0894d14baa9277a431)

As you can see from the table, $z=0$.

Now we can find the reading scale score that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=0$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
0 &= \dfrac {x-221} {36} \\\\
x &= 221
\end{align}$

We could verify the answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.50 \\\\
&\mu:221 \\\\
&\sigma:36 \\\\
x&=221
\end{align}$

The answer:

The maximum reading scale score for students to participate in the intervention experiment is $221$ .

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of annual profit at a chain of stores was approximately normal with mean $\mu = \$66{,}000$  and standard deviation $\sigma = \$21{,}000$.

The executives conducted an audit of the stores with the lowest $20\%$ of profits.

**What is closest to the maximum annual profit at a store where the executives conducted an audit?**  
*Round to the nearest thousand dollar.*

[[☃ radio 2]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 66{,}000 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 21{,}000 $.

We want to find the annual profit such that the area under the curve to its left is $0.2$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Profits, in thousands of dollars, = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 18 to 150 in increments of 21. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 18 with increasing steepness to its peak at 66, before falling with decreasing steepness through 150, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 50 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6dc69379645e12a1df3123ef0a00cd1a6764d2d0)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.2$ below it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately negative 0.85 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6cae8810c681b91d4e615dbb29aee1e2a9a2b2aa)

We can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, negative 0.9, negative 0.8, and negative 0.7, and 3 columns labeled, 0.04, 0.05, and 0.06. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.1736, 0.1711, 0.1685. Row 2. 0.2005, 0.1977, 0.1949. Row 3. 0.2296, 0.2266, 0.2236. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/82a3cffd6080c2257276540e95d961d4c47d3399)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = negative 0.85. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at negative 0.85 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.1977.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/81a77a852f7a0abd7a39d1ae8cfafd9b954a6053)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx -0.85$ to guarantee that we are in the bottom $20\%$.

Now we can find the annual profit that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=-0.85$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
-0.85 &= \dfrac {x-66{,}000} {21{,}000} \\\\
x &= 48{,}150
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.20 \\\\
&\mu:66000 \\\\
&\sigma:21000 \\\\
x&=48326
\end{align}$

The answer:

The maximum annual profit at a store where the executives conducted an audit is approximately $\$48{,}000$.

assessment_items.question_content:
The distribution of durations for which apartments remain empty after the resident moves out for one property management company over the past $10$ years was approximately normal with mean $\mu = 85$ days and standard deviation $\sigma = 29$ days.

The property management company tags the files of the apartments that were empty for the shortest $5\%$ of durations to have priority cleaning the next time their residents move out.

**What is the maximum duration for which an apartment remained empty for the company to tag its file?** 
*Round to the nearest whole number.*  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Consider the normal distribution with mean $ \mu = 85 $  and standard deviation $ \sigma = 29 $.

We want to find the duration such that the area under the curve to its left is $0.05$.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, Days empty = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 31 to 201 in increments of 29. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 31 with increasing steepness to its peak at 85, before falling with decreasing steepness through 201, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately 35 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e963280f2916ab9b3eadb1463846d1b793dfebea)

First, we must determine the $z$-score with an area of $0.05$ below it.

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = unknown. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at approximately negative 1.65 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/91e3d4e5bdea2f1640ba8f71bde77a0896009cdb)

We can use the standard normal probabilities table to determine the approximate $z$-score we are looking for.

![A standard normal probabilities table has 3 rows labeled, negative 1.7, negative 1.6, and negative 1.5, and 3 columns labeled, 0.04, 0.05, and 0.06. Data is as follows. Row 1. 0.0409, 0.0401, 0.0392. Row 2. 0.0505, 0.0495, 0.0485. Row 3. 0.0618, 0.0606, 0.0594. Row 2 and column 2 are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a5b0b508c0929edeb596927dae07a8fe88760e1b)

![A normal distribution curve is plotted along a horizontal axis labeled, z = negative 1.65. The axis ranges from negative 4 to 4 in increments of 1. The curve rises from the horizontal axis at negative 4 with increasing steepness to its peak at 0, before falling with decreasing steepness through 4, then appearing to plateau along the horizontal axis. All values estimated. A dashed vertical line segment rises from the horizontal axis at negative 1.65 to the curve. The area beneath the curve, leftward of the dashed line is shaded. An arrow identifies the shaded area = 0.0495.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/76ed06a935506c7ffda5abb38be2d061ceecc812)

As we can see from the table, we should use $z\approx -1.65$ to guarantee that we are in the bottom $5\%$.

Now we can find the duration that corresponds to a $z$-score of $z=-1.65$ by plugging in values and solving for $x$:

$\begin{align}
z &= \dfrac {x-\mu} {\sigma} \\\\
-1.65 &= \dfrac {x-85} {29} \\\\
x &= 37.15
\end{align}$

We could get a more accurate answer by using the inverse normal function on a calculator:

$\begin{align}
&\text{invNorm} \\\\
&\text{area: }0.05 \\\\
&\mu:85 \\\\
&\sigma:29 \\\\
x&=37.2992
\end{align}$

The answer:

The maximum duration for which an apartment remained empty for the company to tag its file is approximately $37$ days.
